title: Pouteria revoluta
intro: Pouteria revoluta, commonly known as the West Indian satinwood, is a tropical tree native to the Caribbean region and parts of Central and South America. The bark and roots of Pouteria revoluta have been traditionally used for their medicinal properties, exhibiting antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial effects, which contribute to its potential health benefits in treating various conditions such as fever, cough, and respiratory issues. The bioactive constituents of Pouteria revoluta include flavonoids, alkaloids, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, the bark and roots of Pouteria revoluta are often used to make teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are used to treat various health conditions, as well as to promote overall well-being.
taxonomy: Pouteria revoluta is classified in the family Sapotaceae, which includes a wide range of tropical and subtropical trees. This family is part of the larger order Ebenales. The genus Pouteria itself is native to the Americas, with Pouteria revoluta specifically found in Central and South America. Pouteria revoluta is a species of sapote, a type of evergreen tree known for its edible fruit. The classification of Pouteria revoluta is further supported by its unique characteristics, such as its leathery leaves and showy flowers.
